turns.content: hey so I need to figure out something with my truck. the check engine light came on again this morning and I do not have time for this

That's always stressful, especially when you're relying on it daily. A check engine light can mean a lot of different things, from something minor like a loose gas cap to something more serious.

Do you have a way to get the code read? Most auto parts stores will scan it for free with an OBD-II reader. That'll tell you exactly what triggered it.

What kind of truck is it, and roughly how many miles? That helps narrow down what's most likely going on.
it is a 2015 F-150. she has about 140,000 miles on her, you know. every single one of those miles on rez roads. I am not exaggerating when I say the roads out here will shake a truck apart

A 2015 F-150 with 140,000 miles is honestly still in its working life for that truck — the 5.0L and 3.5L EcoBoost engines are known to go well past 200k with maintenance. But you're right that reservation roads are brutal. The washboard gravel and potholes put way more stress on a vehicle than highway miles.

At 140k on rough roads, the most common check engine triggers would be:
- **Oxygen sensor** — very common at that mileage, usually $150-300 to replace
- **Catalytic converter efficiency** — rough roads vibrate exhaust components loose
- **EVAP system leak** — could be as simple as the gas cap seal cracking
- **Ignition coil or misfire** — the EcoBoost engines are prone to this

Getting that code read is really the first step. Is there an auto parts store nearby, or are you pretty far from town?

nearest O'Reilly is in Window Rock. that is about an hour from Chinle on a good day. on a bad day with the mud it can take longer. I know every single pothole on that road by now. some of them have been there longer than my truck has

An hour to the nearest parts store — that's a real factor. You don't want to make that trip just to read a code and then have to go back for parts.

A couple of options:
- **Buy a cheap OBD-II scanner** — they're about $20-30 online and plug right into the port under your dash. Worth having on hand so you can read codes yourself anytime without the Window Rock drive
- **Check if the truck is running differently** — is it idling rough, losing power, running hot, or getting worse gas mileage? That can help narrow it down before you make the trip
- **If the light is steady (not flashing)**, it's usually not an emergency. A flashing check engine light means stop driving now

Is the light steady or flashing?

steady. she is running fine honestly. no shaking, no weird sounds, just that light staring at me. the shocks are shot though. have been for a while. I feel every bump between here and the clinic. but that is a different problem

If the light is steady and the truck is running normally, you're probably looking at something like an O2 sensor or EVAP code — annoying but not dangerous. You've got time to deal with it.

The shocks are a different story, especially on those roads. Worn-out shocks on rough terrain mean:
- **Faster wear on everything else** — tires, ball joints, tie rods, bushings all take more punishment when the shocks aren't absorbing impacts
- **Longer braking distance** — the truck bounces instead of staying planted
- **Driver fatigue** — you're absorbing all those bumps yourself, which adds up on a daily commute

For an F-150 at that mileage on rez roads, I'd honestly prioritize shocks over the check engine light. A set of Bilstein 5100s or Rancho RS9000XLs would be a huge upgrade for rough road driving and usually run $400-600 for all four, plus installation.

How far is your daily drive to the clinic?

about 15 minutes on pavement. but I also drive to Window Rock for meetings, Sometimes Tsaile. and when the weather is bad the dirt roads between communities turn into something else entirely. mud season is real out here, you know

So your truck is really covering a range of conditions — some pavement, a lot of dirt and gravel, and full-on mud when the weather turns. That's a demanding daily cycle for any vehicle.

For the shocks specifically, given your roads I'd lean toward the Bilstein 5100s. They're built for exactly this kind of mixed-surface driving:
- Firm enough to handle washboard gravel without bouncing you around
- Good mud performance — keeps the tires in contact with the ground
- Durable enough that they won't wear out in a year on rez roads

For the check engine light — order that OBD-II reader online. They ship to Chinle, right? Once you know the code, you can decide if it's worth the Window Rock trip for parts or if you can order those online too.

One more thing: at 140k miles on rough roads, it's worth getting underneath the truck and checking for anything loose or cracked in the suspension and exhaust. All that vibration can crack exhaust hangers and loosen heat shields, which might even be what triggered the check engine light.

yeah things ship here. takes forever sometimes but they get here. I will order one of those scanner things. and the shocks, you know, I keep saying I will deal with them and then something else comes up. the truck keeps going so I keep driving. she is a warrior

She sounds like one. 140,000 miles of rez roads is no joke — that's probably equivalent to 250,000 highway miles in terms of wear and tear. The F-150 earning its keep.

Here's a priority list so it doesn't feel overwhelming:

1. **Order the OBD-II scanner** — cheap, one-time purchase, saves you the Window Rock trip every time a light comes on
2. **Read the code** — once you know what it is, we can figure out if it's urgent or can wait
3. **Shocks when you can** — not an emergency but the single biggest quality-of-life upgrade for your daily drive

And in the meantime, keep doing what you're doing. If the truck is running fine with a steady light, she'll get you to the clinic and back. Just keep an eye on how she's driving — any change in behavior, that's when to pay attention.

ahéhee. that makes it feel manageable. I will get the scanner ordered tonight after I check on the sheep. always something, you know. truck, clinic, sheep. but the truck is the one that cannot wait because if she goes down I am walking to work

Exactly — the truck is the lifeline. Everything else depends on it getting you where you need to go. Order that scanner, read the code, and go from there. If the code comes back as something specific, feel free to bring it back here and I can help you figure out the next step.

Go check on those sheep and have a good evening. The truck's been a warrior for 140,000 miles — she's not quitting on you tonight.
